preamble: The shift between the pharyngeal fricative [ʕ] (Ayin) and the uvular fricative [ʁ] (Ghayn) is deeply tied to the history of Semitic languages. While Proto-Semitic originally distinguished the two, they merged into [ʕ] in Canaanite languages like Hebrew. However, in certain specific dialectal traditions or liturgical pronunciations, the pharyngeal shifted back toward or was replaced by a uvular fricative.
